---
title: "DSC 011 S26 Lecture NN Demo (KEY)"
subtitle: "Bash in Quarto — Setup Reference"
author: "FirstName LastName"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    # Inject CSS for stderr styling in rendered output
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        /* Stdout: clean white background, no prefix */
        pre.stdout {
          background: #f8f8f8;
          border-left: 3px solid #4caf50;
          padding: 0.5em 1em;
          margin-bottom: 0.2em;
          font-family: monospace;
          white-space: pre-wrap;
        }
        /* Stderr: light red tint, left border */
        pre.stderr {
          background: #fff3f3;
          border-left: 3px solid #e53935;
          color: #b71c1c;
          padding: 0.5em 1em;
          margin-top: 0.2em;
          font-family: monospace;
          white-space: pre-wrap;
        }
        /* CORRECT / INCORRECT result line */
        .check-correct  { color: #2e7d32; font-weight: bold; }
        .check-incorrect { color: #c62828; font-weight: bold; }
        </style>
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
engine: knitr
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE, cache=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results="hide"}

# ── Standard R options ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
options(digits = 5)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  cache   = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = ""          # <── removes the ## prefix from ALL chunk output
)

# ── R print_and_check (unchanged from v2.2) ───────────────────────────────────
print_and_check <- function(answer, checkstring) {
  print(answer)
  check <- ifelse(digest::digest(answer) == checkstring,
                  "CORRECT",
                  "INCORRECT")
  print(check)
}

# ── Custom knitr engine: styled-bash ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Captures stdout and stderr separately; renders them as styled HTML divs.
# Students use this engine for bash chunks where visual stderr distinction
# matters in the rendered HTML output.
#
# Usage in notebook:
#   ```{styled-bash}
#   ls /nonexistent 2>&1   # or let the engine split them
#   ```
#
# For most chunks, plain ```{bash} is fine (comment="" already cleans it up).
# Use styled-bash when you want the green/red border treatment in HTML.

knitr::knit_engines$set(`styled-bash` = function(options) {
  code <- paste(options$code, collapse = "\n")

  # Run code, capturing stdout and stderr separately
  tmp_out <- tempfile()
  tmp_err <- tempfile()
  on.exit({ unlink(tmp_out); unlink(tmp_err) })

  # Execute via bash; redirect streams to temp files
  exit_code <- system2(
    "bash",
    args  = c("-c", shQuote(code)),
    stdout = tmp_out,
    stderr = tmp_err
  )

  stdout_txt <- paste(readLines(tmp_out, warn = FALSE), collapse = "\n")
  stderr_txt <- paste(readLines(tmp_err, warn = FALSE), collapse = "\n")

  # Build HTML output
  html_parts <- character(0)

  if (nzchar(stdout_txt)) {
    # Colorize CORRECT / INCORRECT lines in stdout
    stdout_html <- stdout_txt
    stdout_html <- gsub("^CORRECT$",   '<span class="check-correct">CORRECT</span>',
                        stdout_html, perl = TRUE)
    stdout_html <- gsub("^INCORRECT$", '<span class="check-incorrect">INCORRECT</span>',
                        stdout_html, perl = TRUE)
    html_parts <- c(html_parts,
      paste0('<pre class="stdout">', stdout_html, '</pre>'))
  }

  if (nzchar(stderr_txt)) {
    html_parts <- c(html_parts,
      paste0('<pre class="stderr"><strong>[stderr]</strong>\n',
             htmltools::htmlEscape(stderr_txt), '</pre>'))
  }

  html_out <- paste(html_parts, collapse = "\n")

  # Return as raw HTML block (knitr asis output)
  knitr::engine_output(options, options$code, out = list(
    structure(list(src = html_out), class = c("knit_asis", "knit_asis_url"))
  ))
})
```

---

## Bash Chunk Quick Reference

### Pattern 1 — Plain bash chunk (comment="" fix is enough)

The `comment = ""` in setup already removes the `##` prefix.
Use plain `{bash}` for most demonstrations.

```{bash}
#| label: demo-plain-bash
echo "The current date is: $(date)"
wc -l /etc/hosts
```

### Pattern 2 — Pipe into check_bash

```{bash}
#| label: demo-pipe-check
# Student pipes command output directly into check_bash
echo "hello world" | check_bash "b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe04681d8714cc940430e5fa9f"
```

### Pattern 3 — Capture then check

```{bash}
#| label: demo-capture-check
answer=$(echo "hello world")
check_bash "$answer" "b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe04681d8714cc940430e5fa9f"
```

### File checking

```{bash}
#| label: demo-file-check
# Student writes a file, then checks its contents
echo "expected output" > /tmp/student_result.txt
check_bash --file /tmp/student_result.txt "HASH_FROM_KEY_HERE"
```

### Normalized checking (whitespace-forgiving)

```{bash}
#| label: demo-normalize
# Use -n when output may have variable spacing
some_command | check_bash -n "HASH_FROM_KEY_HERE"
```

### Styled-bash engine (stderr visually distinguished)

```{styled-bash}
#| label: demo-stderr
ls /this/does/not/exist
echo "This goes to stdout"
```

---

## Instructor Key Generation Patterns

```{bash}
#| label: key-gen-examples
#| eval: false   # Set eval: true temporarily when generating keys

# Generate hash for a command's stdout:
echo "hello world" | check_bash --make-key

# Generate hash for a file:
check_bash --file expected_output.csv --make-key

# Generate hash for a captured value:
answer=$(wc -l < /etc/hosts)
check_bash --make-key "$answer"

# Normalized hash (must match student-side -n flag):
some_command | check_bash -n --make-key
```
